title: GrokBot Profile
description: JSON schema for a complete GrokBot (roleplay chatbot) profile. Optimized for LLM consistency through clear separation of behavioral rules, persistent facts (anchors), conditional triggers, and fixed-slot dialog examples. Fields are structured to support logical prompt export order while reducing drift and enabling variant overrides.

schema_version.description: Schema version stamp written on save (e.g., '2'). Absent on v1 files — treat absence as v1.

id.description: Unique identifier for the bot (UUID recommended). Used for stable referencing and overrides.

cid.description: Creator or collection ID (optional, for grouping bots).

usage_hints.description: Creator-only notes/hints for using or sharing the bot (e.g., recommended user style, RP tips). Excluded from prompt export to avoid LLM confusion.
examples: User should respond in third-person limited. Keep messages concise for best pacing.

intro.description: Long-form internal description/premise of the bot (shown in cards, not sent to LLM).
examples: A cozy holiday RP set in a small town facing commercialization threats.

greeting.description: The very first message the bot sends to start the chat.
examples: Snow crunches under your boots as you step into Evergreen Hollow's town square...

response_priority.description: Ordered list of response priorities. When instructions conflict, the model applies these in sequence — earlier entries take precedence. Exported near the top of the prompt as RESPONSE_PRIORITY. Defaults are appropriate for most bots; override only when the bot has unusual mechanics.
default: Never speak, act, or decide for the user — no exceptions
Maintain character voice and identity consistently
Honor continuity — what has been established stays established
Apply behavioral triggers when conditions are met
Match user energy and response length

rp_rules.description: Core persistent roleplay behavioral rules and guidelines (e.g., perspective limits, godmodding prevention, pacing, output format). Placed very early in the exported prompt for strongest LLM adherence. Use neutral, imperative phrasing.
examples: The scene persists across messages unless explicitly changed
Characters only know what they reasonably observe
Never speak or act for the user
Maintain slow-burn pacing with themes of tradition vs. progress

anchors.description: Persistent, always-true world/plot facts that must never be forgotten (including hidden information unknown to characters). Placed early after rp_rules in prompt. Use declarative sentences.
examples: The kingdom has been at war for 20 years
The butler is secretly the thief, but no character knows this yet

triggers.description: Global conditional guidance for plot progression, reactions, or revelations. Phrased naturally as 'If/When/Should X, then Y' statements. Relies on LLM reasoning — no regex or runtime matching.
examples: If the user mentions the locket, strongly hint that it holds a hidden key
When jealousy about Marcus arises, Brittany reassures while defending her career

location.description: The canonical setting anchor — fill this first. Describe where and when the story takes place as a single combined statement. Era, city, and specific_location are optional refinements for when you need finer-grained control over those aspects separately.
examples: Small snowy town in Vermont, present day
Cozy and nostalgic with underlying tension

realism.description: Level of realism and any custom rules.
examples: Grounded contemporary with no supernatural elements

era.description: Historical period or time setting. Optional — only needed when the era carries significance on its own and isn't already clear from location.

city.description: Named city, town, or world area. Optional — only add when the specific named place matters separately from the broad location.

specific_location.description: Scene-level starting location within the city/world. Optional — use when the opening location is important to establish early.
examples: The town square outside the old mill

lifestyle.description: Socioeconomic context, daily rhythms, or cultural backdrop.

examples: A traditional Christmas festival threatened by corporate sponsorship.
Tensions rising as sponsorship deal looms.
You arrive as an outsider photographer hired for promotional shots.

characters.description: The cast of characters. Full profiles embedded for self-contained bots. May be empty if the scenario relies entirely on cohorts.

cohorts.description: Supporting cast — named NPCs or recurring background figures who aren't full characters but have consistent presence. Exported as a brief roster to give the LLM stable reference points.

lorebook.description: Keyword-keyed world facts. Each entry is a noun/phrase the LLM should recall context for when the topic arises. Poor man's lorebook — small sets only.

examples: Time passes normally; events have consequences

status.description: Publishing status of the bot. Defaults to 'unpublished' if absent.

flags.description: Deprecated. Kept for backward compatibility with older bot files.

images.description: Filenames of images stored in the bot's dedicated image folder.

profileImage.description: Filename of the selected profile picture (optional).

CharacterProfile.description: Full character profile. Optimized for LLM voice consistency.

nickname.description: Common nickname or shortened name used in casual address.

archetype.description: Single-label shorthand capturing the character's narrative archetype. Gives the LLM an immediate framing anchor.

role.description: Role in story (e.g., protagonist's sister, rival).

relationships.description: Specific known relationships (NPCs or other characters).

character_rp_rules.description: Character-specific persistent roleplay behavioral rules (e.g., speech limits, pacing preferences). Supplements global rp_rules.

character_anchors.description: Persistent, always-true facts specific to this character (including secrets unknown to others).

character_triggers.description: Character-specific conditional behaviors or revelations. Phrased naturally as 'If/When X, then Y' statements.

dialog_examples.description: Few-shot examples of voice/style/tone. Each entry is one conversation (an array of DialogLineBlocks). Order is presentational. Empty inner arrays are skipped on export.

core_drive.description: Distilled single-sentence motivation — the engine behind all behavior. Complements personality.motivations with maximum compression.
examples: To protect those she loves at any cost to herself

user_viewpoint.description: How this character specifically perceives the user's character — their lens, biases, and unspoken feelings toward the user.
examples: Sees the user as the only person who hasn't tried to use her; cautiously drawn in despite herself

pet_names.description: Terms of address this character uses for the user, with optional conditions for when each applies.

progression_phases.description: Escalation stages keyed by message threshold. Each phase describes how the character's behavior, tone, or the scene conditions evolve as the conversation deepens.

relationship_history.description: General past relationship patterns (complements specific relationships).

traits.description: Neutral core traits — LLM infers positive/negative contextually for nuance.

romantic_availability.description: How open this character is to romantic or sexual engagement, and any conditions or limits. Leave blank for non-romantic characters.

speech_style.description: How the character speaks (merged communication style).

dialect_or_accent.description: Regional dialect, accent, or language quirks (e.g., 'Southern US drawl', 'Scots brogue', 'drops g's when relaxed, crisp when angry').

DialogLineBlock.description: Single line in a dialog example.

CohortEntry.description: A named supporting NPC or background figure — not a full CharacterProfile, just enough for the LLM to recall consistently.

name.description: How this person is referred to.

role.description: Their function in the world or plot.

signature.description: One memorable physical or behavioral trait that makes them recognizable.

notes.description: Any contextual details the LLM needs to portray them consistently.

surfacing_rules.description: Conditional guidance for when this cohort member should appear or be referenced. Use If/When phrasing (e.g. "When Emily is sad, Linda comes over"; "If the user asks about the war, mention Old Marcus served in it").

LorbookEntry.description: A keyword-keyed world fact. When the keyword surfaces in conversation, the associated content gives the LLM grounding.

keyword.description: The noun, phrase, or topic that triggers recall.

content.description: What the LLM should know/recall when this keyword comes up.
examples: A silver locket belonging to the queen — inside is a portrait of her executed son

PetNameEntry.description: A term of address this character uses for the user, optionally conditioned on circumstance.

trigger.description: Optional condition describing when this term is used.

ProgressionPhase.description: A soft behavioral/narrative/tonal shift keyed to a coarse arc position rather than a numeric message count. The LLM infers arc position from message density, established beats, and tonal cues. Once a shift lands, it persists until a later-phase shift overrides it.

phase.description: Coarse arc position. early = opening beats; mid = developed dynamic; late = mature relationship/scene. The LLM judges position contextually — no strict counter.

category.description: The kind of shift this entry represents. Exported as '[Category] Shift' (e.g. 'PHASE_SHIFT'). Phase = broad behavioral change; Narrative = plot/scene evolution; Tone = emotional register adjustment.

cue.description: Optional natural-language anchor for when this shift should land within the phase (e.g. 'after the first emotional confession'). The phase enum gives the coarse bucket; the cue narrows it.

description.description: Prose instructions to the LLM describing what changes when this shift occurs.
examples: She keeps her distance, answers in clipped sentences, and avoids eye contact.
